title: Penetration Test Finding Severity and Re-test Status

compliance.assurance_rationale: PCI DSS 11.4.5 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that PCI DSS 11.4.5 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.11.76: Penetration Test Finding Severity and Re-test Status.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.11.76' running on index=grc sourcetype="grc:penetration_test" — finding_id, severity, status, retest_date, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

spl:
index=grc sourcetype="grc:penetration_test" earliest=-365d
| where match(engagement_scope,"(?i)CDE|payment|PCI)")
| where match(status,"(?i)open|accepted_risk")
| eval overdue_retest=if(isnotnull(retest_date) AND now()>retest_date,1,0)
| stats values(severity) as sev, max(overdue_retest) as overdue by finding_id, title
| where match(mvjoin(sev,","),"(?i)critical|high") OR overdue=1
| sort finding_id
description: Tracks open penetration test findings and re-validation dates so critical issues from annual or incremental tests are remediated within agreed timelines and evidence is ready for PCI validation.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for PCI DSS compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes grc and sourcetypes grc:penetration_test. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Normalize severity strings. (2) Link `finding_id` to Jira epics. (3) Monthly steering committee view. (4) Store signed pentest report hash in GRC with Splunk snapshot. Indexes required: `index=grc`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=grc:penetration_test`.
visualization: Kanban-style table (status), timeline (retest_date), single value (open critical count). Key fields: `finding_id`, `overdue`, `overdue_retest`, `sev`, `title`.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.user, Authentication.action, Authentication.src | sort - count
dataModelAcceleration: Enable acceleration for Authentication (CIM data model).
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorised penetration testing** — Annual or more frequent penetration tests conducted by QSA-approved assessors against cardholder data environment (CDE) systems produce expected security alerts that are pre-communicated and tracked via the test engagement letter.

2. **PCI ASV scanning activity** — Quarterly external vulnerability scans performed by Approved Scanning Vendors generate network probe events that trigger IDS/IPS alerts as an expected part of the PCI DSS validation process.

3. **Tokenization system batch processing** — The payment tokenization platform performs scheduled batch re-tokenization and token lifecycle management operations that produce high-volume cardholder data access patterns within approved operational windows.

4. **Acquirer and processor settlement activity** — End-of-day settlement reconciliation between the merchant platform and payment processor involves legitimate PAN/SAD data transmission that is covered by the processor service provider agreement.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `pci_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved pen-test engagement IDs, ASV scan source IPs, scheduled batch job names, and settlement processing windows. The search filters matches where `pci_exception_valid=true` and the exception has not expired.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `grc` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `11.4.5`. The control objective is: "Evidence that PCI DSS 11.4.5 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.11.76: Penetration Test Finding Severity and Re-test Status." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `grc` under `grc:penetration_test`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


Four distinct operational considerations shape this detection's rollout:


• **Hint 1:** Normalize severity strings. Field normalisation mismatches are one of the top silent-failure modes in enrichment-driven detections — invest in automated tests that assert key-match rates after every TA upgrade.

• **Hint 2:** Link `finding_id` to Jira epics. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** Monthly steering committee view. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** Store signed pentest report hash in GRC with Splunk snapshot. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

This search aggregates `values()`, `max()` across the last year, grouped by `finding_id, title`. For "Penetration Test Finding Severity and Re-test Status," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the PCI DSS control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time. `values()` collects distinct values into a multivalue field per group; `list()` preserves order and duplicates. For compliance evidence, `values()` is preferred — the auditor cares what values appeared, not how many times.

Why aggregate at search time rather than rely on raw events: compliance reporting requires summarisation to be defensible and stable. A raw-event dashboard can show different counts depending on the exact second it was queried; an aggregated result rounded to the UC's schedule produces consistent numbers across the reporting period. If the downstream consumer (GRC platform, regulator submission) expects stable daily/weekly figures, schedule this search to write to a summary index and read from the summary for reports — that way the numbers quoted on Monday match what was quoted on Wednesday.

Cardinality vigilance: if `finding_id, title` has very high cardinality (e.g. grouping by a free-text `user_agent` or `url` field), the result set explodes and the search becomes slow. Check cardinality with `| stats dc(finding_id) as cardinality` before scheduling; if >100000 distinct groups, either narrow the search, pre-filter to the top N values, or redesign the search to aggregate at a coarser grain.

**CIM / accelerated variant:** The detection also has an accelerated variant using `| tstats` against the CIM `Authentication` datamodel:

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.user, Authentication.action, Authentication.src | sort - count
```

When to prefer this variant: once the underlying datamodel is accelerated (verify in Settings > Data Models > `Authentication` > status=100%), this query runs 10-50x faster than the raw variant and can feasibly cover multi-month windows for audit reporting. When to avoid: if your field extractions are app-specific and the CIM tagging is incomplete, the `tstats` variant will silently under-count. Always compare raw vs accelerated counts on a known day before swapping the production schedule. Maintain both variants in the UC — the raw is the primary for accuracy, the accelerated is the performance optimisation for dashboarding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.11.76: Penetration Test Finding Severity and Re-test Status]
cron_schedule = 0 */6 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -365d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.11.76,reg=PCI_DSS
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=grc sourcetype=grc:penetration_test earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `finding_id`, `overdue`, `overdue_retest`, `sev`, `title`. Run:

```spl
index=grc sourcetype=grc:penetration_test earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table finding_id, overdue, overdue_retest, sev, title
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.11.76: PCI DSS evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Kanban-Style Table (Status) (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Time trend (`retest_date`):** Place in the main content row. Use `timechart span=1d` aggregated appropriately for the regulation's review cadence (daily for PCI/HIPAA, weekly for ISO-style controls). Annotate the chart with control-change dates so auditors can see when mitigations took effect.

**Panel 3 — Single-value KPI (`open critical count`):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'PCI DSS current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Search returns no results when violations are expected:** Verify time-window alignment, index existence, and field extractions in that order. Most 'missing results' are simpler configuration issues before they're detection-logic issues.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**PCI DSS-specific failure modes:**

• Scope creep: PCI Requirement 1.2 requires CDE (Cardholder Data Environment) documentation. Every PCI DSS detection must explicitly reference the CDE inventory lookup; an 'all hosts' search that happens to catch CDE hosts is NOT compliant evidence — the QSA needs to see the scope boundary enforced in the query itself.

• Log retention vs online availability: Req 10.5.1 requires 1 year of audit log retention with at least 3 months immediately available. The index's `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` must be >= 31536000; additionally, Splunk's summary index or an accelerated datamodel must cover the last 90 days for immediate auditor retrieval. One-year hot/warm is not required; the distinction matters for cost.

• Daily log review (Req 10.4): PCI requires DAILY review evidence. Detections backing this control must produce signed evidence per calendar day, not per 24-hour rolling window — a search scheduled at 23:55 daily is compliant; one running every 24h from an arbitrary start time is not.

• FIM (File Integrity Monitoring, Req 11.5.2) scope: FIM detections must cover critical system files on CDE devices, not 'all changes anywhere.' Use the CDE host list as the first-pass filter; flags on non-CDE hosts dilute the signal and are out of scope for the RoC.
grandmaExplanation: We track open penetration test findings and re-validation dates so critical issues from annual or incremental tests are remediated within agreed timelines and evidence is ready for PCI validation. PCI DSS is about protecting card and payment data when we run these checks.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Inject a synthetic test event that satisfies the detection criteria for 'Penetration Test Finding Severity and Re-test Status' — for example, create a log entry with all required fields populated to match the SPL filter conditions. Verify the search fires within the expected schedule interval, the alert populates the compliance dashboard, and the event routes correctly to the evidence index with proper PCI DSS control tagging.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Ingest a batch of test events that are structurally similar but explicitly do NOT meet the detection threshold — e.g., events with compliant values, timestamps outside the monitoring window, or source systems excluded by the scope filter. Verify the search produces zero results, no false-positive alerts fire, and the compliance posture score remains unaffected for the PCI DSS control objective.

evidence: Saved search results archived to the `audit_evidence` index with PCI DSS control tagging; scheduled PDF/CSV exports for auditor consumption; Splunk dashboard screenshots with timestamp watermarks capturing control operating effectiveness over the review period.
exclusions: Excludes test/sandbox environments, pre-production systems, development instances without regulated data, and any systems formally documented as out-of-scope in the PCI DSS programme scope statement approved by the control owner.
